Title: Biosynthesis of alkanes/alkenes from fatty acids or derivatives (triacylglycerols or fatty aldehydes)

Common_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Increases free fatty acid supply for downstream alkene biosynthesis, thereby supporting alkene production.
Common_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. Genetically modified TesA from Escherichia coli was paired with OleTMC in a de novo alkene biosynthesis pathway in modified E. coli. 2. TesA converted acyl-ACPs into free fatty acids, supplying substrate to the decarboxylase step. 3. In a separate microbial platform study, an E. coli thioesterase similarly increased FFA supply for UndA-dependent medium-chain α-olefin production.
Common_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Heterologous expression in engineered microbial strains; pathway reconstruction; hydrocarbon/alkene product profiling
Common_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Classified as Common Gene because it supplies precursor FFAs rather than directly catalyzing terminal hydrocarbon-forming decarboxylation/decarbonylation or acting as a regulator.
Common_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Useful as a precursor-supply module to combine with fatty acid decarboxylases for microbial hydrocarbon production.

Common_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Improves electron transfer to OleTMC and increases alkene production.
Common_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. CamA was cloned with CamB into modified E. coli harboring OleTMC and TesA. 2. CamA functioned as a putidaredoxin reductase to overcome electron transfer limitations in OleTMC. 3. This engineering increased alkene production by 2.8-fold.
Common_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Pathway engineering in E. coli; co-expression with partner proteins; alkene quantification
Common_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Accessory redox partner, not the principal terminal-step enzyme.
Common_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Useful redox module for improving activity of P450 fatty acid decarboxylases in engineered microbes.

Common_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Improves electron transfer to OleTMC and increases alkene production.
Common_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. CamB was cloned with CamA into modified E. coli harboring OleTMC and TesA. 2. CamB acted as putidaredoxin in the OleTMC electron transfer chain. 3. Together with CamA, it helped raise alkene production by 2.8-fold.
Common_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Pathway engineering in E. coli; co-expression with partner proteins; alkene quantification
Common_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Accessory redox partner, not the principal terminal-step enzyme.
Common_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Useful electron-transfer component for supporting P450 decarboxylase-based alkene synthesis.

Common_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Acts as an efficient heterologous redox partner to support OleTJE-mediated decarboxylation of FFAs to α-olefins.
Common_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. SeFdx-6 was reported as an efficient heterologous redox partner for OleTJE decarboxylation activity. 2. It supports H2O2-independent electron transfer systems for fatty acid decarboxylation. 3. Such redox support facilitates more practical α-olefin production.
Common_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Coupled enzyme system; heterologous redox partner testing; in vitro decarboxylation assays
Common_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Accessory ferredoxin rather than the focal hydrocarbon-forming enzyme.
Common_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Valuable supporting component for building H2O2-independent OleTJE systems.

Common_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Acts as an efficient heterologous reductase partner to support OleTJE-mediated α-olefin production.
Common_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. CgFdR-2 was identified as an efficient heterologous redox partner for OleTJE activity. 2. It participates in H2O2-independent electron delivery for fatty acid decarboxylation. 3. It improves practicality of enzymatic α-olefin production when paired with appropriate ferredoxin.
Common_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Coupled enzyme system; heterologous redox partner testing; in vitro decarboxylation assays
Common_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Accessory reductase rather than terminal hydrocarbon-forming enzyme.
Common_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Useful reductase module for engineered OleTJE-based production systems.

Common_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: When fused to OleTJE, enables self-sufficient O2/NADPH-driven α-olefin production without auxiliary redox partners.
Common_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. The reductase domain of P450BM3 was genetically fused to OleTJE. 2. The fusion allowed O2-dependent and NADPH-driven linear α-olefin production without separate redox partners. 3. The resulting self-sufficient biocatalyst showed promise for scalable α-olefin production.
Common_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Gene fusion; biocatalytic assay; product profiling
Common_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Electron-transfer/support module in fusion enzyme, not itself the focal hydrocarbon-forming catalytic gene in this review.
Common_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Useful engineering component for self-sufficient P450 decarboxylase systems.

Common_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Converts fatty acids to aldehydes, supplying substrate for downstream aldehyde decarbonylase-mediated alkene production.
Common_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. CAR from Mycobacterium marinum was expressed in E. coli with a cyanobacterial aldehyde decarbonylase. 2. It reduced aliphatic fatty acids to aldehydes. 3. This enabled production of fatty alkenes (C7-C15) through the combined pathway.
Common_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Heterologous pathway expression; hydrocarbon product analysis
Common_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Upstream precursor-supplying enzyme rather than the direct hydrocarbon-forming decarbonylase.
Common_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Useful aldehyde-supply module for hydrocarbon biosynthesis pathways.

Common_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Supplies fatty aldehydes for aldehyde decarbonylase-dependent alkane production.
Common_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. A fatty acid reductase from Photorhabdus luminescens was combined with a Nostoc punctiforme aldehyde decarbonylase. 2. The reductase converted FFAs into fatty aldehydes in engineered E. coli. 3. This supported in vivo production of linear and branched-chain alkanes.
Common_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Heterologous pathway engineering; in vivo alkane production assay
Common_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Precursor-generating enzyme rather than the direct alkane-forming step.
Common_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Useful upstream module for constructing synthetic alkane pathways.

Pathway_Genes.Applied_Species: E. coli; in vitro biocatalytic systems; Saccharomyces cerevisiae
Pathway_Genes.Enzyme_Name: fatty acid decarboxylase / cytochrome P450 peroxygenase
Pathway_Genes.Pathway_Branch_or_Subpathway: Free fatty acid oxidative decarboxylation to terminal alkenes
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Oxidatively decarboxylates free fatty acids to one-carbon-shorter terminal alkenes (α-olefins) and CO2.
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Biofuel hydrocarbons, including biogasoline/green diesel/biojet fuel precursors
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Promotes production of long-chain α-olefins from free fatty acids.
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. OleTJE is highlighted in the review as the most studied and one of the most promising fatty acid decarboxylases for biological hydrocarbon production. 2. It directly oxidatively decarboxylates long-chain FFAs into terminal α-olefins under mild conditions. 3. H2O2-dependent and H2O2-independent systems, fusion designs, redox partner coupling, and microbial expression platforms all improved practical α-olefin production. 4. Its major limitation is narrow specificity toward long, linear saturated FFAs, often yielding long-chain products that may require downstream upgrading.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Heterologous expression; purified enzyme assays; decarboxylation assays; redox partner coupling; fusion-protein engineering; microbial metabolic engineering; product profiling
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Heterologously expressed in E. coli and Saccharomyces cerevisiae
Pathway_Genes.Applied_Promoters: Optimized promoter/plasmid combinations in Saccharomyces cerevisiae were reported, specific promoters not given in this text
Pathway_Genes.Interacting_Proteins: SeFdx-6; CgFdR-2; P450BM3 reductase domain; flavodoxin; flavodoxin reductase; alditol oxidase
Pathway_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: H2O2-dependent or NADPH/O2-dependent conditions; atmospheric pressure; near-ambient temperature
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Narrow substrate specificity; tendency to produce long-chain α-olefins requiring cracking and/or saturation; H2O2 can inactivate or denature enzyme; possible competing hydroxylation side reaction
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: High value biocatalyst for hydrocarbon biofuel production; can be improved by redox partner engineering, fusion design, immobilization/co-immobilization, and substrate-scope engineering.
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Although primarily a decarboxylase, OleTJE can also perform β-hydroxylation as a secondary reaction; branch-point between monooxygenation and decarboxylation remains important. Review paper: enzyme summarized across multiple systems rather than a single experiment.

Pathway_Genes.Applied_Species: in vitro biocatalytic systems; Yarrowia lipolytica; Bacillus subtilis spore system
Pathway_Genes.Pathway_Branch_or_Subpathway: Photodecarboxylation of free fatty acids and triacylglycerol-derived fatty acids
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Uses blue light and FAD to photodecarboxylate fatty acids into hydrocarbons, mainly alkanes.
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Drop-in biofuels such as diesel-like hydrocarbons
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Promotes light-driven hydrocarbon production from fatty acids or lipid-derived fatty acids.
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. CvFAP is a key photoenzyme highlighted for blue-light-driven decarboxylation of FFAs into hydrocarbons. 2. It was also applied to triacylglycerol/FFA conversion in cascade systems with lipases, achieving near-complete hydrolysis/decarboxylation in some one-pot systems. 3. Heterologous expression in Yarrowia lipolytica enabled production of diesel-like hydrocarbons, with output strongly dependent on light intensity. 4. Engineering of substrate channel/access by decoy molecules was explored to broaden applicability.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Purified enzyme photobiocatalysis; blue-light illumination assays; whole-cell catalysis; lipase-coupled cascades; heterologous expression in Yarrowia lipolytica; hydrocarbon profiling
Pathway_Genes.Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step: Key terminal hydrocarbon-forming step in light-driven pathway
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Heterologously expressed in Yarrowia lipolytica and assembled on Bacillus subtilis spores
Pathway_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Blue light; light intensity strongly affects productivity; high blue light can impair host viability
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Requires light input; long-chain substrate preference; high light intensity can cause photo-inactivation or impair host physiology; product titers may remain low in some systems
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Promising for light-driven drop-in biofuel production, particularly in lipid-accumulating hosts or lipase-coupled systems; optimization of light regime and substrate access is critical.
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Uses a hydrophobic substrate tunnel leading to FAD; mechanistically distinct from OleT-type P450 decarboxylases.

Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Converts fatty aldehydes into one-carbon-shorter alkanes with formate as coproduct in an oxygen-dependent, unusual redox-neutral mechanism.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Promotes alkane formation from fatty aldehydes.
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. The cyanobacterial aldehyde decarbonylase from Nostoc punctiforme is highlighted as a representative enzyme for biological alkane production from aldehydes. 2. The review summarizes evidence that it forms alkanes with formate coproduct, not CO, via an unusual oxygen-dependent mechanism. 3. It requires a reducing system and molecular oxygen, and its low activity can limit practical application. 4. It has been used in engineered microbial pathways to produce linear and branched-chain alkanes and biojet-range alkanes.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Biochemical mechanistic studies; in vitro product analysis; isotopic/mechanistic studies summarized in review; heterologous pathway engineering in E. coli
Pathway_Genes.Interacting_Proteins: ferredoxin; ferredoxin oxidoreductase; NADPH-dependent reducing system; fatty acid reductase from Photorhabdus luminescens
Pathway_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Requires molecular oxygen and external reducing system; activity affected by oxygen availability and inhibitory effects of ferredoxin system
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Very low activity; substrate solubility limitations; instability; inhibition associated with ferredoxin reducing system
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Important terminal alkane-forming enzyme for synthetic biology, but requires stabilization and activity improvement for industrial deployment.
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Review notes that a C-terminal degron appears common in bacterial aldehyde decarbonylases and may be a target for stabilization engineering.

Pathway_Genes.Applied_Species: Pseudomonas platform; Pseudomonas aeruginosa; Acinetobacter baylyi ADP1
Pathway_Genes.Pathway_Branch_or_Subpathway: Medium-chain free fatty acid oxidative decarboxylation to α-olefins
Pathway_Genes.Primary_Substrate: medium-chain free fatty acids, especially lauric acid
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Oxidatively decarboxylates medium-chain free fatty acids into one-carbon-shorter terminal α-olefins.
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Hydrocarbon biofuel molecules and industrial chemicals
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Promotes medium-chain α-olefin production from FFAs, especially conversion of lauric acid to 1-undecene.
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. UndA is summarized as the gene responsible for oxidative decarboxylation of medium-chain FFAs to α-olefins. 2. Biochemical and structural analyses suggested an unusual β-hydrogen abstraction mechanism. 3. It was deployed in microbial platforms, including Pseudomonas-based systems and Acinetobacter baylyi ADP1, for one-step α-olefin production from fatty acid or glucose/ferulate-derived intermediates.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Biochemical and structural analyses; heterologous expression in microbial platforms; hydrocarbon product profiling
Pathway_Genes.Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step: Key terminal hydrocarbon-forming step for medium-chain α-olefins
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Heterologously expressed in Pseudomonas aeruginosa and Acinetobacter baylyi ADP1
Pathway_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Ferulate used as sole carbon source in one engineered Acinetobacter system
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Useful medium-chain α-olefin-forming enzyme for microbial fuel production from sugars or lignin-derived compounds.
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: The review specifically emphasizes medium-chain substrate preference and unusual mechanism.

Pathway_Genes.Pathway_Branch_or_Subpathway: Free fatty acid oxidative decarboxylation to α-olefins
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Catalyzes oxidative decarboxylation of free fatty acids to α-olefins.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Supports α-olefin production in a lipolytic microbial platform.
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. UndB was cloned and expressed together with UndA in Pseudomonas aeruginosa. 2. The resulting lipolytic microbial platform produced α-olefins in a one-step process. 3. The review cites it as another fatty acid decarboxylase used in microbial hydrocarbon production.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Gene cloning and expression in microbial platform; product analysis
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Potential complementary decarboxylase for one-step microbial α-olefin biosynthesis.
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Source species not explicitly stated in the provided text, so left as NA.

Pathway_Genes.Pathway_Branch_or_Subpathway: De novo alkene biosynthesis from fermentable sugar via FFA intermediate
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Decarboxylates free fatty acids to alkenes in engineered microbial pathways.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Enables de novo alkene production in modified E. coli.
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. OleTMC from Macrococcus caseolyticus was used in a designed de novo alkene biosynthesis pathway in modified E. coli. 2. It was paired with TesA to match substrate specificity. 3. Overcoming electron transfer limitations with CamA/CamB increased alkene production by 2.8-fold.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Metabolic engineering in E. coli; co-expression with TesA and CamA/CamB; alkene quantification
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Useful decarboxylase for synthetic de novo microbial alkene production, especially when paired with matched thioesterase and redox partners.
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Review summarizes pathway-level engineering rather than detailed enzyme mechanism.

Pathway_Genes.Pathway_Branch_or_Subpathway: Free fatty acid oxidative decarboxylation to α-olefins
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Decarboxylates free fatty acids into α-olefins, supported by in situ H2O2 generation in cascade systems.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Improves α-olefin production in glucose oxidase-coupled systems relative to exogenous H2O2 feeding.
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. OleTSA was used instead of OleTJE in a cascade with glucose oxidase for endogenous H2O2 generation. 2. The process efficiency improved relative to exogenous H2O2-based systems. 3. OleTSA was also used in co-immobilized form with glucose oxidase on polymeric nanoparticles for hydrocarbon production from triacylglycerol-based feedstocks.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Cascade biocatalysis; co-immobilization; comparative activity/product assays
Pathway_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: In situ H2O2 generation from glucose oxidation
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Susceptibility of heme-containing decarboxylases to oxidative damage from H2O2 remains relevant
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Alternative decarboxylase for engineered H2O2-managed α-olefin production systems.
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Emphasized in cascade/co-immobilization context rather than as the principal review centerpiece.
